title: US-Iran Ceasefire Shattered as Both Sides Trade Strikes Over Hormuz
subhead: The US struck Iranian missile and drone sites after Iran hit a cargo ship in the Strait of Hormuz, while oil prices plunged below $70 and Iraq threatened to quit OPEC. The Iran-Lebanon-Israel flashpoint also shifted with a new framework deal.

sections.heading: US Strikes Iran After Drone Attack on Cargo Ship in Hormuz
sections.body:
The US military struck Iranian missile and drone storage locations and coastal radar sites on Friday, in retaliation for an Iranian drone attack on the cargo vessel M/V Ever Lovely transiting the Strait of Hormuz near Oman the day before. President Trump called the drone strike a 'foolish violation of our ceasefire agreement,' noting that one drone 'solidly hit the upper deck' while three others were shot down. US Central Command said the strikes lasted roughly an hour.

Iran's Revolutionary Guards (IRGC) claimed they repelled the US attack on Sirik Island near the strait and immediately retaliated, striking 'the locations where the terrorist US military is stationed in the region.' The IRGC warned: 'If the aggression is repeated, our response will be broader than this.' An Iranian port official told Mehr news agency that Sirik port suffered no damage and was operating normally. Iranian parliament national security chief Ebrahim Azizi wrote that the Strait of Hormuz 'is governed by Iran' and that Iran's actions constituted 'ceasefire management, not violation.'

The incident underscores a fundamental clash over who controls Hormuz transit. The IRGC insists only Iran-designated routes are authorized; the US, the IMO, and Oman established an alternative corridor hugging the Omani coast. Iran's foreign ministry reiterated that the strait should be jointly governed by Iran and Oman, while Ali Akbar Velayati, adviser to the supreme leader, warned Gulf states their survival 'depends on Tehran's tolerance.' (NPR, ABC Australia, Rappler)
sections.whyItMatters: The ceasefire's core dispute is now explicit: Iran claims sovereign control over Hormuz transit rules, while the US treats any attack on commercial shipping as a ceasefire violation. Until this is resolved, every transit is a potential flashpoint.

sections.heading: Hormuz Reopening Hits Reverse as IMO Suspends Evacuation Mission
sections.body:
The International Maritime Organization suspended its coordinated evacuation program for roughly 500 stranded ships and 11,000 seafarers still trapped in the Persian Gulf, after the Ever Lovely was struck while using the IMO's Oman-corridor route. About 115 vessels had been evacuated before the pause, IMO secretary-general Arsenio Dominguez said.

Transit data had been improving before the attack. Wednesday saw 78 vessel transits, the highest since the war began, and 85 additional transits on Tuesday and Wednesday combined, according to a Joint Maritime Information Center report. Kpler recorded 57 commodity crossings on Wednesday, falling to 42 on Thursday after the drone strike. At least two tankers reversed course after Iran insisted only its designated routes could be used. Lloyd's List editor-in-chief Richard Meade warned the surge was 'a ceasefire-driven release of pent-up demand — a ketchup-bottle burst of tonnage,' adding: 'The Strait of Hormuz may be busier, but it is not safer.'

Secretary of State Marco Rubio issued a joint statement with the Gulf Cooperation Council calling for 'free, unconditional, and unrestricted navigation' through the strait, explicitly opposing tolls or control assertions. (ABC Australia, NPR, Stripes, JMIC report)
sections.whyItMatters: The IMO suspension means 500 ships remain stranded. Every day of delay costs carriers and insurers millions and keeps global supply chains distorted. The data shows how quickly confidence evaporates when violence returns.

sections.heading: Oil Prices Crash Below $70 as Glut Signals Emerge
sections.body:
Brent crude fell 4.3% to settle below $72 a barrel and WTI dropped 3.7% to near $69 on Friday, erasing nearly all wartime gains. The Brent contango widened to its biggest since 2023, a classic oversupply signal. The price crash happened even amid military escalation — a sign that markets are pricing in a flood of returning Gulf supply.

Saudi Aramco resumed crude loadings at Ras Tanura, the world's biggest oil port, after a near-four-month halt. Bloomberg calculations show overall Gulf exports at about 75% of prewar levels. Saudi Arabia is expected to slash August official selling prices for Asia by up to $8/barrel, to a four-month low, according to a Reuters survey. Iraq, Kuwait, and the UAE are all boosting output.

But the supply surge is running into a tanker shortage. Iraq was forced to halt production at one key field due to the shortage of available vessels. And Rystad Energy chief economist Claudio Galimberti cautioned: 'It will take time for production to ramp back up, for logistics to normalise, and for the risk premium embedded in crude prices to dissipate.' (LA Times, Reuters, Bloomberg, Rystad Energy)
sections.whyItMatters: Oil's plunge amid active combat is remarkable. The market is betting the reopening holds, but the tanker bottleneck and continued attacks on shipping mean the physical supply recovery is far less smooth than the price chart suggests.

sections.heading: Iraq Threatens OPEC Exit as Post-UAE Cartel Erosion Accelerates
sections.body:
Iraq has formally considered leaving OPEC if the cartel does not raise its production quota, Reuters reported, citing sources with knowledge of the matter. The threat comes just weeks after the UAE exited the group. On Friday, Iraq's Oil Ministry said OPEC has begun 'gradually restoring' its prewar production allocations — but Baghdad wants more.

The IEA projects UAE output will reach 5.2 million bpd in 2027, up 730,000 bpd, after its OPEC exit. Iraq's calculus is similar: war losses need to be recouped, and the cartel's quota system is blocking recovery. An Iraqi oil ministry statement later walked back the exit threat, saying it was 'not the government's official position,' but the signal is clear.

Meanwhile, the UAE is fast-tracking construction of a new pipeline to Fujairah on the Gulf of Oman, bypassing Hormuz for crude — though not for LNG or refined products. As Francis Perrin of IRIS noted: 'For refined products and LNG, if the Strait of Hormuz is blocked, you are blocked 100 percent.' (Reuters, IEA, Al Monitor)
sections.whyItMatters: Two OPEC members threatening or executing exits in rapid succession signals the cartel's cohesion is under real strain. If Iraq leaves, OPEC's ability to manage global supply — already weakened by the UAE departure — erodes further.

sections.heading: UK Seizes Russian Shadow Fleet Tanker in English Channel
sections.body:
Royal Marine Commandos boarded the Smyrtos, a Cameroon-flagged Russian shadow fleet tanker, in a six-hour operation in the English Channel on June 15 — the first such seizure by UK armed forces. National Crime Agency officers arrested an Indian national, 38, on suspicion of sanctions offences. The 24 Georgian and Indian crew are assisting the investigation. The vessel, which previously sailed under a different name and flag, was sanctioned in July 2025 and departed Russia's Ust-Luga port on June 5.

Prime Minister Keir Starmer called the operation 'yet another blow to Russia' and a reminder 'that we will not let them hide.' The MoD said Russia's shadow fleet of more than 700 vessels carries 75% of its sanctioned oil. The boarding was enabled by new powers Starmer announced in March allowing UK forces to board sanctioned vessels in British waters.

The seizure coincided with a turbulent week for UK defence policy: defence secretary John Healey and armed forces minister Al Carns both resigned, warning the defence investment plan fell 'well short' and was 'neither transformative enough nor sufficiently funded.' (BBC, UK MoD)
sections.whyItMatters: The first-ever UK military seizure of a shadow fleet tanker marks an escalation in enforcement against Russian oil sanctions evasion. But the resignation of the two top defence officials underscores the tension between operational ambition and funding reality.

sections.heading: Israel-Lebanon Framework Deal Signed, Hezbollah Declines
sections.body:
The US, Israel, and Lebanon signed a trilateral framework agreement on Friday aimed at ending the Israel-Hezbollah conflict. Secretary of State Rubio called it 'the beginning of the beginning,' while Netanyahu declared it a 'great achievement,' insisting Israel would remain in a security zone in southern Lebanon until Hezbollah is disarmed.

Lebanon's ambassador to the US, Nada Hamadeh, framed the deal as enabling 'our people to go back to their land.' But Hezbollah said it would not cooperate with the disarmament provisions. The agreement represents a deliberate effort to delink Lebanon's fate from the broader US-Iran negotiations — a significant diplomatic choice that Tehran is unlikely to welcome.

The timing is notable: the deal was announced on the same day the US struck Iran, signaling Washington's strategy of pursuing confrontation with Tehran and conciliation with Beirut simultaneously. (NPR, NYT, Rappler)
sections.whyItMatters: The framework attempts to separate the Lebanon track from the Iran track — a key diplomatic bet. If Hezbollah refuses to disarm, the deal collapses. If it holds, it could reshape the regional conflict by removing one front from Iran's leverage.

sections.heading: G7 Critical Minerals Alliance Takes Shape as China Halts Rare Earth Exports to Japan
sections.body:
At the G7 summit in Evian, leaders established a strategic alliance on critical minerals to reduce dependence on China, which controls over 90% of global rare earth production. Japan's PM Takaichi proposed a joint stockpiling partnership during the working dinner.

Canada and Japan are already moving bilaterally: trade minister Maninder Sidhu told Reuters the two countries are exploring joint critical mineral stockpiling and mining projects. Over C$1 billion ($705 million) in commercial deals were signed during Sidhu's visit to Japan, including expansion of the Mitsubishi-backed LNG Canada Phase 1 project. The G7 declaration expressed concern about 'arbitrary export restrictions' — a direct reference to China halting rare earth exports to Japan amid their escalating dispute.

Three Japanese shipbuilders — Imabari, Kawasaki Heavy Industries, and Namura — announced plans to revive domestic LNG carrier construction by 2035, aiming for 3-5 vessels per year with possible South Korean technology partnerships. The move reflects Tokyo's urgency to secure energy transport independence after the Hormuz shock. (Oilprice, Reuters, SCMP, Nikkei)
sections.whyItMatters: China's rare earth export ban to Japan has turned the G7's critical minerals rhetoric into action. The Japan-Canada stockpiling plan and Japan's LNG carrier revival are concrete responses to the Hormuz shock — the supply chain restructuring is now moving from debate to contracts.

bottomLine: The US-Iran ceasefire is under its most serious strain: Iran hit a cargo ship transiting Hormuz, the US struck back, and the IRGC retaliated against US positions in the region. The IMO suspended its ship evacuation program, leaving 500 vessels stranded. Oil crashed below $70 even as fighting resumed — markets are pricing a supply glut, but the tanker shortage and transit risks tell a different story. Iraq's threat to quit OPEC follows the UAE's exit and signals accelerating cartel erosion. A US-Israel-Lebanon framework deal tries to separate the Lebanon track from Iran, but Hezbollah's non-cooperation leaves its viability uncertain. The G7 critical minerals alliance is becoming operational just as China weaponizes rare earth exports against Japan.
sources: NPR, ABC Australia, Rappler, LA Times, BBC, UK MoD, Stripes, Reuters, Bloomberg, Al Monitor, SCMP, Nikkei, Oilprice, NYT, JMIC, Kpler, Lloyd's List, Rystad Energy, IRIS, IMO, UKMTO, Iran Mehr News, CENTCOM
